content:
# 2 Corinthians Chapter 4 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/2CO_004_page_001.jpg

1. Therefore, having this ministry by the mercy of God, we do not lose heart.

2. But we have renounced disgraceful, underhanded ways. We refuse to practice cunning or to tamper with God's word, but by the open statement of the truth we commend ourselves to everyone's conscience in the sight of God.

3. And even if our gospel is veiled, it is veiled to those who are perishing.

4. In their case the god of this age has blinded the minds of the unbelievers, to keep them from seeing the light of the gospel of the glory of Christ, who is the image of God.

5. For what we proclaim is not ourselves, but Jesus Christ as Lord, with ourselves as your servants for Jesus' sake.

6. For God, who said, "Let light shine out of darkness," has shone in our hearts to give the light of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ.

7. But we have this treasure in jars of clay, to show that the surpassing power belongs to God and not to us.

8. We are afflicted in every way, but not crushed; perplexed, but not driven to despair;

9. persecuted, but not forsaken; struck down, but not destroyed;

10. always carrying in the body the death of Jesus, so that the life of Jesus may also be manifested in our bodies.

11. For we who live are always being given over to death for Jesus' sake, so that the life of Jesus also may be manifested in our mortal flesh.

12. So death is at work in us, but life in you.

13. Since we have the same spirit of faith according to what has been written, "I believed, and so I spoke," we also believe, and so we also speak,

14. knowing that he who raised the Lord Jesus will raise us also with Jesus and bring us with you into his presence.

15. For it is all for your sake, so that as grace extends to more and more people it may increase thanksgiving, to the glory of God.

16. So we do not lose heart. Though our outer self is wasting away, our inner self is being renewed day by day.

17. For this light momentary affliction is preparing for us an eternal weight of glory beyond all comparison,

18. as we look not to the things that are seen but to the things that are unseen. For the things that are seen are transient, but the things that are unseen are eternal.

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Chapter 4 contains Paul's theology of weakness at its most concentrated — the "jars of clay" passage that is the thesis of the entire letter.

**Verse 6 — Creation theology:** All three translators recognized the Genesis 1:3 allusion. SOPHIA affirms: Paul's identification of conversion with creation is not metaphorical but ontological — the same God, the same creative word, the same movement from darkness to light.

**Verses 8-9 — The hardship catalogue:** All three translators preserved the four-pair structure. SOPHIA affirms the rhetorical power of the compressed form.

---
## ⚠️ Disagreement Log

### v.1 — "having this ministry" vs. "since God in his mercy has given us this new way"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "having this ministry, as we received mercy" | Preserves participial structure and separate mercy clause |
| LOGOS | "having this ministry by the mercy of God" | Combines into one phrase |
| PNEUMA | "since God in his mercy has given us this new way" | "New way" replaces "ministry"; full restructuring |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA's "new way" is not a rendering of διακονία ("ministry/service") — it imports a concept from chapter 3 that Paul does not use here.

**Status:** Resolved. PNEUMA replaces a key Pauline term.

---

### v.2 — "hidden things of shame" vs. "disgraceful, underhanded ways" vs. "shameful deeds and underhanded methods"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "renounced the hidden [things] of shame, not walking in craftiness nor adulterating the word of God" | Literal; preserves three distinct clauses |
| LOGOS | "renounced disgraceful, underhanded ways. We refuse to practice cunning or to tamper with God's word" | Two sentences; "tamper" for δολοῦντες |
| PNEUMA | "reject all shameful deeds and underhanded methods. We don't try to trick anyone or distort the word of God" | "Trick anyone" — adds object not in Greek |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. "Tamper" preserves the sense of δολόω (to adulterate/falsify). PNEUMA's "trick anyone" adds a personal object not present in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved. Minor additions in PNEUMA.

---

### v.4 — "god of this age" vs. "god of this world" vs. "Satan, who is the god of this world"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the god of this age" | αἰών as "age" (temporal) |
| LOGOS | "the god of this world" | αἰών as "world" (spatial/temporal) |
| PNEUMA | "Satan, who is the god of this world" | Identifies "the god" as Satan explicitly |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the god of this age" — combining EMET's temporal precision with LOGOS's smooth phrasing. αἰών is primarily temporal ("age/epoch"), not spatial ("world"). PNEUMA's identification of "Satan" is exegetically correct but makes explicit what Paul leaves implicit.

**Status:** Resolved. "Age" vs. "world" is a meaningful distinction — Paul's thought is temporal/eschatological.

---

### v.4 — "image of God" vs. "exact likeness of God"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "image of God" | εἰκών as "image" |
| LOGOS | "image of God" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "exact likeness of God" | Adds "exact" to εἰκών |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "image of God" (EMET/LOGOS). "Exact" is an interpretive addition — εἰκών means "image/likeness" without specifying degree. PNEUMA imports from Hebrews 1:3 (χαρακτήρ, "exact imprint").

**Status:** Resolved. PNEUMA imports precision not in this text.

---

### v.5 — "slaves" vs. "servants"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "your slaves because of Yeshua" | δούλους as "slaves" — primary meaning |
| LOGOS | "your servants for Jesus' sake" | "Servants" — standard English rendering |
| PNEUMA | "your servants for Jesus' sake" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "servants" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). While δοῦλος literally means "slave," "servants" is the established English convention in this context and avoids the modern connotations of chattel slavery that differ from ancient servitude.

**Status:** Resolved. Recurring δοῦλος debate — "slave" vs. "servant."

---

### v.6 — "Out of darkness light shall shine" vs. "Let light shine out of darkness" vs. "Let there be light in the darkness"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Out of darkness light shall shine" | Follows Greek word order |
| LOGOS | "Let light shine out of darkness" | Echoes Genesis 1:3 phrasing |
| PNEUMA | "Let there be light in the darkness" | Closer to Genesis 1:3 LXX wording |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Paul's phrasing is not a direct quotation of Genesis 1:3 but an allusion. LOGOS captures the imperative force while maintaining the darkness-to-light movement. PNEUMA's "Let there be light" is closer to Genesis but further from Paul's actual Greek.

**Status:** Resolved. All three capture the creation allusion; phrasing differs.

---

### v.7 — "earthen vessels" vs. "jars of clay" vs. "fragile clay jars"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "earthen vessels" | ὀστρακίνοις σκεύεσιν — archaic but precise |
| LOGOS | "jars of clay" | Modern English equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "fragile clay jars" | Adds "fragile" to emphasize vulnerability |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "jars of clay" (LOGOS). "Earthen vessels" (EMET) is archaic. PNEUMA's "fragile" is an addition — while contextually accurate, the Greek does not include an adjective for fragility; that quality is inherent in the material.

**Status:** Resolved. PNEUMA adds interpretive adjective.

---

### v.10 — "the dying of Yeshua" vs. "the death of Jesus" vs. "the death of Jesus" (via suffering)

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the dying of the Yeshua" | νέκρωσιν as "dying" (process) — distinct from θάνατος ("death" as event) |
| LOGOS | "the death of Jesus" | Standard rendering; loses process nuance |
| PNEUMA | "Through suffering, our bodies continue to share in the death of Jesus" | Adds "through suffering"; restructured |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS ("the death of Jesus") for readability, but EMET's note is important: νέκρωσις means "dying" (an ongoing process) rather than θάνατος ("death" as completed event). Paul carries Jesus' dying — his suffering is a continuous participation in Christ's death. This nuance is noted but not reflected in the consensus text for clarity.

**Status:** ⚠️ Theologically significant. νέκρωσις ("dying"/process) vs. θάνατος ("death"/event) — EMET captures a distinction lost in LOGOS and PNEUMA. The consensus text sacrifices this nuance for readability.

---

### v.12 — "death works in us, but life in you"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "death works in us, but life in you" | Terse, literal |
| LOGOS | "death is at work in us, but life in you" | Nearly identical; "is at work" |
| PNEUMA | "So we live in the face of death, but this has resulted in eternal life for you" | Adds "eternal"; restructures |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "eternal" — Paul says "life" (ζωή), not "eternal life." The terse paradox is deliberate and should not be expanded.

**Status:** Resolved. PNEUMA adds "eternal" not in the text.

---

### v.16 — "outer person/inner person" vs. "outer self/inner self" vs. "bodies/spirits"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the outer person of us is being destroyed...the inner [person] of us is being renewed" | ἔξω ἄνθρωπος / ἔσω as "outer/inner person" |
| LOGOS | "our outer self is wasting away, our inner self is being renewed" | "Self" for ἄνθρωπος |
| PNEUMA | "our bodies are dying, our spirits are being renewed" | Identifies outer=body, inner=spirit |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA's "bodies...spirits" is an interpretation — Paul says ἄνθρωπος ("person/human"), not σῶμα ("body") or πνεῦμα ("spirit"). The outer/inner person distinction is anthropological, not a simple body/soul dualism.

**Status:** ⚠️ Theologically significant. PNEUMA imports a body/spirit dualism that Paul's language does not require. The "outer person" includes more than the body; the "inner person" is not simply the spirit.

---

### v.17 — "momentary lightness of affliction" vs. "light momentary affliction" vs. "present troubles are small"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the momentary lightness of the affliction of us is producing for us an eternal weight of glory beyond all measure" | Preserves "lightness" (ἐλαφρόν) and "weight" (βάρος) contrast |
| LOGOS | "this light momentary affliction is preparing for us an eternal weight of glory beyond all comparison" | "Light" as adjective; "weight" preserved |
| PNEUMA | "our present troubles are small and won't last very long. Yet they produce for us a glory that vastly outweighs them and will last forever!" | Full paraphrase; two sentences |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The "light/weight" wordplay is central — Paul contrasts ἐλαφρόν (light, weightless) with βάρος (weight, heaviness), and this connects to the Hebrew kavod/kaved etymological pun. PNEUMA's "small" loses the weight imagery entirely.

**Status:** Resolved. Theologically noteworthy — the light/weight contrast is a deliberate rhetorical device.

---

### v.18 — "temporary" vs. "transient" vs. "soon be gone"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the [things] being seen [are] temporary" | πρόσκαιρα as "temporary" |
| LOGOS | "the things that are seen are transient" | "Transient" for πρόσκαιρα |
| PNEUMA | "the things we see now will soon be gone" | Temporal paraphrase |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. "Transient" captures the fleeting quality. PNEUMA's "soon be gone" adds temporal specificity ("soon") not in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved. Minor word choice.

---

*Summary: 12 disagreements logged. Two flagged ⚠️: v.10 (νέκρωσις "dying" vs. "death" — process vs. event) and v.16 (outer/inner "person" vs. body/spirit dualism). Other significant: v.4 ("age" vs. "world"), v.5 ("slaves" vs. "servants"), v.7 ("earthen vessels" vs. "jars of clay"), v.17 (light/weight wordplay).*

— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA
